description: This figure presents the variation in normalized elastic modulus (δE = E/E₀ [%]) as a function of true plastic strain for SPCE sheet steel, measured using a precision extensometer. The scatter plot displays experimental data points (diamonds) together with a fitted polynomial curve given by E = E₀*(351ε² - 160ε + 93.8). As true strain increases from 0 to approximately 0.25, the relative elastic modulus decreases monotonically from 100% to roughly 72–75%. The accompanying text identifies the material as SPCE and attributes this pronounced decrease in apparent elastic modulus to changes in dislocation structure—specifically dislocation pile-up at grain boundaries and other obstacles during unloading and sub-yield reloading—rather than to density reduction, which remains below 1%. This strain-dependent degradation in elastic modulus is central to the paper’s conclusion that incorporating the observed modulus variation improves the accuracy of spring-back prediction in forming simulations.

Figure 2 is a line graph comparing experimental and simulated spring-back values, plotted in degrees against bend angle before unloading (160° to 90°) for SPCE sheet metal in a V-bending test. The plot contains three series: experimental measurements marked by diamonds, finite element simulations with constant elastic modulus shown as squares, and simulations with varied elastic modulus represented by triangles. Visually, the experimental spring-back rises from roughly 0.9° at 160° to about 1.4° at 90°. The constant-E simulation yields the lowest values, increasing gradually from approximately 1.0° to 1.15°, thereby systematically underpredicting the experimental spring-back across the entire angle range. The varied-E simulation produces higher values, from about 1.15° up to roughly 1.35°–1.4°, tracking the experimental trend considerably more closely than the constant-E approach. The figure supports the conclusion that incorporating the reduction in apparent elastic modulus caused by plastic deformation—where the material’s stiffness decreases with prior straining—substantially improves spring-back prediction accuracy compared to conventional constant-modulus simulations.

This figure presents a schematic model illustrating dislocation pile-up at a grain boundary in plastically deformed mild steel (SPCE and SPCC). Panel (a) shows a sequence of edge dislocations accumulating on a slip plane as they are blocked by the grain boundary on the right, with an applied shear stress τ indicated by the horizontal arrow. Panel (b) depicts the corresponding dislocation sources emitting dislocations that glide toward the grain boundary, where dashed arrows trace their trajectories from source to pile-up. The caption identifies this as a dislocation pile-up model, and the cited text explains that such pile-up near grain boundaries creates a greater concentration of movable dislocations in these regions than in the grain interior. Consequently, the apparent elastic modulus varies spatially from the grain boundary to the grain center. This schematic supports the central hypothesis that plastic-deformation-induced dislocation mobility reduces the apparent elastic modulus, providing the mechanistic basis for the nano-indentation experiments used to measure intragranular modulus distributions.

Figure 4 presents paired mechanical property charts for SPCC sheet steel, showing (a) stress–strain curves measured with a strain gauge up to 1 % strain and (b) the corresponding variation in the elastic modulus (stress–strain gradient) versus strain. The five curves labeled A–E represent distinct processing histories identified in the text: as-received, 15 % tensile pre-strained, pre-strained followed by low-temperature heat treatment (373 K for 90 h), annealed, and as-received with heat treatment. In (a), the pre-strained specimens display pronounced nonlinearity and a progressively decreasing slope within the nominally elastic region, while the annealed specimen maintains the highest stress level and the most linear response. Panel (b) quantifies this degradation: the 15 % pre-strained condition shows a continuous drop in modulus from initial loading, whereas the pre-strained and heat-treated specimen exhibits partial recovery where the gradient initially decreases and then rises toward the annealed level. The annealed specimen retains a high, nearly constant modulus (approximately 224 GPa initially, 206 GPa overall) until yielding. These trends confirm that the apparent elastic modulus reduction arises from mobile dislocations, and that low-temperature heat treatment can restore modulus stability via Cottrell atmosphere reformation.

This figure presents a schematic diagram (Fig. 5) of the nano-indentation apparatus used to evaluate localized elastic modulus variations in plastically deformed sheet metals. The setup integrates a central microscope equipped with a 3-D piezo actuator that positions an indenter tip against the sample surface, coupled with a transducer for force and displacement sensing. Two parallel control and acquisition chains are depicted: a scanning probe microscopy (SPM) subsystem (SPM controller, SPM computer, and video display monitor) and a transducer subsystem (transducer controller, transducer computer, and video display monitor). These loops are interconnected through a central signal adaptor that coordinates piezo scanning, indentation depth control, and transducer readout. As described in the accompanying context, this instrument configuration enabled nano-indentation measurements on SPCE and SPCC steel specimens to investigate grain-level modulus distributions and the reduction in apparent elastic modulus induced by plastic deformation and dislocation pile-up near grain boundaries.

This figure presents a typical nano-indentation load-displacement (P-h) curve obtained during spherical tip indentation of steel specimens. The graph plots applied load (P) against indentation depth (h), displaying distinct loading and unloading paths. The loading curve shows a smooth, nonlinear increase in load with penetration depth, while the unloading curve descends from the maximum load P_max back toward the depth axis. A dashed tangent line drawn to the initial portion of the unloading curve marks the contact stiffness S = dP/dh, which is the critical parameter for calculating reduced elastic modulus and hardness via the Oliver-Pharr method. This curve relates to the investigation of plastic-deformation-induced property changes in SPCE and SPCC sheet steels. The unloading slope provides quantitative evidence for local elastic modulus variations near grain boundaries and within individual grains, supporting the core conclusion that apparent elastic modulus decreases after plastic deformation due to mobile dislocation activity.

This atomic force microscopy (AFM) image displays the surface topography of a chemically mechanically polished (CMP) SPCE steel specimen near a grain boundary, overlaid with three parallel arrays of nano-indentation measurement points. Line #1 (triangles), Line #2 (diamonds), and Line #3 (squares) are positioned across a ~12 μm horizontal span, corresponding to the X-direction scan at three distinct Y positions. The topographic contrast reveals a darker groove-like feature near the center, consistent with a grain boundary ditch that was partially eliminated by CMP treatment. These measurement arrays, spaced at approximately 1 μm intervals, were used for spherical-tip nano-indentation to map the microscopic elastic modulus distribution. The figure provides the spatial framework demonstrating that the indented region spans from one grain across the boundary into the adjacent grain, supporting the finding that the elastic modulus near the grain boundary is locally decreased compared to the grain interior in plastically deformed steel.

Figure 8 shows a nano-indentation load–penetration curve plotting load P in micro-newtons [μN] against penetration depth h in nanometers [nm]. The curve displays multiple incremental loading–unloading cycles in a sawtooth pattern, reaching a peak load of roughly 380 μN at approximately 10 nm penetration. The caption identifies this as a load–displacement curve, and the citing text reports that it was measured with a spherical indenter of nominal radius R = 50 μm on an SPCE steel specimen. The text further states that the indentation depth was only several nanometers and that a chemical etching and CMP surface treatment was applied to avoid interference from nanometer-scale ditches at grain boundaries. These data are used to determine local elastic properties across grain structures, supporting the conclusion that the elastic modulus near grain boundaries is lower than in adjacent grain regions.

This figure presents three nano-indentation line-scan subplots showing the local elastic modulus Er (in GPa) as a function of position (0–12 μm) across grain boundaries in plastically deformed SPCE steel. Each subplot corresponds to one of three parallel measurement traverses, with a bold arrow marking the grain boundary location. In the top scan, the modulus is approximately 200 GPa in the left grain, drops to roughly 150 GPa at the boundary near 5–6 μm, and recovers to 200–250 GPa in the right grain. The middle scan shows a similar depression to about 150 GPa at the boundary near 6 μm, with the right grain reaching 250–300 GPa. The bottom scan exhibits the most pronounced reduction, with the modulus falling to near 50 GPa at the boundary around 7–8 μm before rising to approximately 250 GPa in the adjacent grain. These profiles demonstrate that the elastic modulus is locally minimized at grain boundaries, while neighboring grains exhibit distinct baseline modulus levels attributed to different crystallographic orientations. The data support the conclusion that plastic deformation causes a decrease in the apparent elastic modulus near grain boundaries.

This figure presents a schematic layout of positional alignment markers on an SPCE sheet-metal specimen prepared for nano-indentation characterization near grain boundaries. Four diamond-shaped marks—produced by a Vickers hardness test machine—are arranged collinearly with precise geometric offsets of 300 μm, 340 μm, and 300 μm from a reference edge positioned at 1150 μm horizontally and 2660 μm vertically. According to the caption, the two larger outer markers are used to find the indentation region after surface treatment, whereas the two smaller inner markers determine the exact measurement point. The specimen surface was etched, marked, and subsequently chemically mechanically polished (CMP); these retained benchmarks enable reliable re-localization of the targeted grain boundary following CMP. This marker arrangement is therefore critical for correlating post-polishing nano-indentation modulus data with specific microstructural features.

This figure comprises an optical micrograph of the SPCE sheet steel measurement region before chemical mechanical polishing (CMP) treatment and a schematic of the nano-indentation measurement grid. The left panel shows the grain structure with a superimposed X–Y coordinate system (origin O) for spatial referencing. The right panel illustrates the planned indentation points arranged in a rectangular array with 1 μm unit spacing along both X and Y axes (X=0–2+ and Y=0–2 shown). This micrograph was used to locate grain boundaries and define precise indentation positions for evaluating the microscopic distribution of elastic modulus near grain boundaries after plastic deformation up to 25% true strain. The marked points facilitate correlation between indentation depth profiles and microstructural features, supporting the conclusion that the elastic modulus decreases near grain boundaries due to dislocation pile-up. The figure serves as the pre-CMP reference for subsequent nano-indentation mapping.

Figure 12 presents nano-indentation measurements of Young's modulus (E_r) distribution near a grain boundary in plastically deformed SPCE sheet steel. The composite plot contains three sub-panels labeled Y=0, Y=1, and Y=2, representing parallel scan lines across the grain boundary region. The horizontal axis shows Position X from 0 to 20 μm, and the vertical axis gives E_r in GPa. Data are plotted as pink squares, blue diamonds, and yellow triangles, with annotations indicating R=50 μm and R=200 μm, likely referring to different indenter radii or measurement conditions. All three scan lines reveal a pronounced local minimum in Young's modulus around X ≈ 5–10 μm, corresponding to the grain boundary location, where values drop to roughly 100–140 GPa compared with 200–280 GPa in the grain interior. The Y=0 line shows the deepest reduction, while Y=1 and Y=2 maintain higher baseline values, reflecting grain-to-grain orientation differences. These spatially resolved results support the conclusion that plastic deformation reduces the apparent elastic modulus specifically near grain boundaries, consistent with dislocation pile-up mechanisms.
